era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Mesopotamia. The Old Kingdom of Egypt (Dynasty IV–V) is in its mature phase; the Great Pyramids of Giza are already centuries old. In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic Period III is underway, with competing city-states (Lagash, Umma, Uruk, Ur) vying for regional dominance. The Indus Valley Civilisation is in its early phases in South Asia. Writing systems (cuneiform, hieroglyphics) are well-established but literacy remains restricted to scribal elites.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
East Asia (Chinese Bronze Age chronology for this period remains debated)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence sparse)

events.title: Reign of Menkaure (Dynasty IV Egypt) — later phase or succession uncertainty
events.description: The reign of Menkaure, builder of the third pyramid at Giza, is traditionally dated to c. 2490–2472 BCE, but absolute chronology for the Old Kingdom remains contested. By 2885 BCE, if this date is accurate, Menkaure would have been long dead and Egypt would be in Dynasty V. However, Egyptian chronology for this period has a margin of error of ±50–100 years depending on the method (radiocarbon, king lists, astronomical calculations). This entry reflects the uncertainty inherent in Old Kingdom dating.
key_figures: Menkaure (Pharaoh, Dynasty IV — if reign extends to this period)

sources.name: Manetho's Aegyptiaca (preserved in Josephus and Eusebius)

events.certainty_note: Old Kingdom Egyptian chronology is notoriously uncertain. Radiocarbon dating, king-list lengths, and astronomical calculations (Sothic cycles) produce dates ranging across a 100+ year window. 2885 BCE is within the plausible range for late Dynasty IV or early Dynasty V, but the specific year is not independently verifiable.

events.title: Lagash–Umma conflict (Early Dynastic Mesopotamia) — ongoing territorial disputes
events.region: Mesopotamia (southern Iraq, Lagash and Umma city-states)
events.description: The city-states of Lagash and Umma engaged in a series of conflicts over control of the Gu'edena (border region) throughout the Early Dynastic III period. Enmetena of Lagash (c. 2404–2375 BCE, conventional dating) is famous for his victory stele commemorating a triumph over Umma. By 2885 BCE, if this date is accurate, such conflicts would have been ongoing for centuries, though specific events for this exact year are not attested. The pattern of inter-city-state warfare is well-documented archaeologically and epigraphically.
key_figures: Enmetena of Lagash (ruler, if reign overlaps)
Lugalzagesi of Umma (rival ruler, conventional dating c. 2354–2335 BCE)
events.certainty_note: The Lagash–Umma conflicts are well-attested, but absolute dating of individual rulers and events is uncertain by ±50–100 years. The year 2885 BCE falls within the Early Dynastic III period, but no specific event is documented for this exact year.

disconfirming_evidence: The absolute chronology of the Old Kingdom of Egypt and Early Dynastic Mesopotamia is contested. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' (dates shifted down by 100+ years), while others defend a 'high chronology.' Radiocarbon calibration and Sothic cycle calculations have produced conflicting results. The year 2885 BCE as assigned here may be off by a century or more. Additionally, the significance of events in this year cannot be verified because no contemporary sources explicitly date events to this specific year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2885 BCE is heavily dependent on king lists (Turin, Manetho) compiled centuries or millennia after the events they describe, and on archaeological stratigraphy and radiocarbon dating, which have their own margins of error. Egyptian sources are biased toward royal and religious narratives; administrative and economic records are sparse. Mesopotamian cuneiform texts are more abundant but often undated or dated by regnal years of rulers whose absolute chronology is uncertain. The absence of contemporary written records for most of the world outside Egypt and Mesopotamia means that major developments in the Indus Valley, China, and elsewhere are invisible in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Old Kingdom Egyptian political structure
